data.title: Sinclair: Value Creation Time For Shareholders
data.description:
We were recently buyers of Sinclair, Inc. (NASDAQ:SBGI) shares on the sharp pullback after earnings, on the belief that we were purchasing shares at an extremely attractive valuation ahead of industry M&A. The timing of those purchases could not have been better for a trader, as news broke that Nexstar (NXST) and Tegna (TGNA) were in discussions to merge, which saw the entire sector rise, followed by news that Sinclair itself was beginning a comprehensive review of its business, which pushed shares higher the next day.

Sinclair issued a press release, which had the headline 'Sinclair Launches Comprehensive Strategic Review for Broadcast Business' but also stated that the Company was going to "simultaneously evaluate separation of Ventures". We believe that this is really good news for shareholders and could be the event many have been waiting for to unlock significant value.

The Backstory

Since the Diamond Sports RSN debacle, Sinclair has focused its assets into two "silos"—one with the legacy broadcast assets and the other housing investments that were related to the TV business, as well as private equity and real estate plays. Management has often discussed how the "ventures" segment had a lot of hidden value and that they were now focusing on this area of the company's holdings to help unlock value for shareholders. While effort has been put into the Tennis Channel (we would note that the Company did lose the French Open rights) to modernize it and offer it as a DTC product, the run-off of private equity investments has generated cash and allowed management to build a sizeable cash reserve ($393 million as of Q2 2025) to make future investments with but also figure out the path forward for the Company as a whole.

During the quarterly conference call, the management team again stressed that the cash and investment portfolio at Ventures had a book value at quarter's end of $726.4 million, of which $392.6 million was cash. With 45,871,818 Class A shares and 23,775,056 Class B shares outstanding, the book value on Ventures alone is $10.42/share, with over half of that in easy-to-access liquid cash/cash equivalents. We would note that if the book value of The Tennis Channel and other operating assets controlled by Ventures were included, the book value would surpass Sinclair's current market cap. However, we are not sure that the value of The Tennis Channel has held up since the Company acquired it nearly 10 years ago.

While we do think that there is some upside from the book values listed, we would note that management did indicate that the Bally's book value was mark-to-market and thus, when combined with cash, shows the current value of over $500 million of Venture's $726.4 million investment portfolio. In short, the gains that we suspect exist for the Real Estate, Direct Investments, and Private Equity/Venture Capital line items are probably significant on a percentage basis for those categories, but for the entire portfolio, it certainly appears that investors have decent estimates for valuations.

The Path Forward

We see three distinct possibilities that Sinclair could choose to pursue moving forward:

  1. Split the Company into two entities, one focusing on broadcast television and the other on venture investments.
  2. Keep the Company together, but utilize the current cash from Ventures to do M&A for broadcast television.
  3. Sell the broadcast business off—either in whole or piecemeal—to the highest bidders.

While the first option would fit the traditional activist investor playbook, we believe that it would create too much confusion in the market and be a major distraction for the Company during what could be one of the last M&A cycles in the industry. Because of this, we think the Company needs to pass on the first option to take a more pragmatic approach to the current M&A cycle within the broadcast industry. (While we understand the recent press release seemed to indicate that this was the likely path, we do not see the scale on that side of the business to justify a split.)

With Nexstar and Tegna agreeing to merge, it leaves a handful of entities with controlling shareholders looking for merger partners—yet the problem is that most have controlling shareholders that appear focused on continuing to be involved. Gray Media (GTN) and EW Scripps (SSP) find themselves in the same position as Sinclair, and we know that Gray views themselves as a buyer, not a seller. If Sinclair now views themselves as a buyer and not a seller too, then we believe that Sinclair will need to utilize the cash from the Ventures side of the company to engage in M&A—either alone or teaming up with another partner—to find a target to buy and split the assets in the most beneficial way to optimize the deal's economics for the buyers.

Depending on the target and depending on the deal structure, we can envision a few scenarios for Sinclair where they could pay the seller a premium multiple but also create economics that allow the deal to be both accretive and deleverage the balance sheet in the near term. If, for instance, Gray and Sinclair were able to convince Scripps to do a deal, the two buyers would have some firepower to work with as their average monthly per-subscriber retransmission fee works out to roughly 30% above Scripps. These rates get adjusted upward after an acquisition to the acquirer's rate, and it is this deal accretion that we see providing the most flexibility for Sinclair to keep leverage in check while still offering attractive terms to interested sellers.

Never Say Never

While Sinclair certainly appears to be one of the companies able to consolidate the industry (and not likely to be sellers, or at least net sellers), the possibility does exist that the Smith family decides that now is the time to sell and looks for a deal that sees them give up control but maintain a sizeable stake while also deleveraging the newly created entity. This could be done by Sinclair selling to Gray with a third party involved (PE-backed, most likely) to take control of stations that overlap, have less appealing economics, or pose antitrust issues by kicking cash into the deal. Accepting a mix of cash and stock helps with the tax hit and putting cash into shareholders' pockets, but it also would allow the newly formed entity to either maintain or improve leverage ratios, as retransmission fee accretion would be minimal, and deal economics would be driven mostly by cost-cutting and other efficiencies. With the Smiths taking a mix of stock and cash, it also allows Gray's controlling family to maintain control while also using the Ventures' cash to pay down the Sinclair debt they will inherit and refinance.

M&A Just Getting Started

Regardless of what has already happened in the industry, we believe that the next year or two will see a lot of additional transactions in this space. There are private equity funds eager to buy in, other funds eager to finance deals, and numerous station groups analyzing what deals make the most sense for them moving forward. While we believe that one of the three family-controlled broadcasters will sell during this window, it is quite possible that all three remain independent and move forward by sourcing smaller deals; rather than a transformative acquisition, they will each have to grind it out wheeling and dealing with other operators.

Final Thoughts

We generally dislike sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) discussions on assets that are carried on the books at historical prices, and management makes arguments about "hidden value". At Sinclair, there is a lot less guessing as to the accuracy of the argument, as a large portion of the Ventures investment portfolio now sits in cash and cash equivalents, with the Bally's investment being marked-to-market. While it is entirely possible that The Tennis Channel and its new DTC properties have seen their value fall since being purchased a decade ago, if we look at the fact that there is roughly $5.64 per share in cash within the Ventures segment, we think that alone provides plenty of upside (potentially 50%) for the shares if deployed properly for M&A activities.

If Sinclair struggles to find a dance partner and is forced to "go it alone", we think a mix of share buybacks and select debt repurchases should be utilized. While some may argue that the Company should focus exclusively on debt repurchases in that scenario, we would note that the cash should currently generate enough in either interest income or earnings credits to offset roughly half of the debt expense (Sinclair has two bonds of note with coupons of 8.125% and 9.75%, and we think they can generate roughly 3.75-4.25% at the bank via earnings credits or interest income via short-term cash instruments with their cash). So share repurchases at current levels are about as attractive as debt repurchases and most likely have a greater impact on helping increase the company's share price.

We remain buyers of Sinclair and the industry as a whole, as we believe the current M&A window will help the entire industry work out its leverage/balance sheet issues and cut out duplicative expenses both at the corporate level as well as the local level, where two stations can be owned.

We prefer to buy shares below the $15.50/share level, but for long-term buyers, value all the way up to $17.50/share.

data.title: AMD: This Is Your Exit Opportunity
data.description:
Earlier this month, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) reported decent Q2 results, which showed modest revenue growth and strong profitability. However, the latest developments within the semiconductor industry suggest the market is overly optimistic about the company’s ability to continue to aggressively grow in the near term. There are already signs that the data center sales are slowing, the gaming business is underperforming, and the AI server capabilities may not scale quickly enough to offset cyclical headwinds in other segments. At the same time, rising geopolitical risks could also undermine future growth. Because of that, we continue to remain bearish about AMD and stick with our Sell rating for the company.

The Market Has Become Overly Excited

To this day, AMD’s data center segment remains the company’s key driver of growth, and in Q2, it generated $3.2 billion in revenues, up 14% Y/Y. While the segment continues to grow at a double-digit rate, the growth rate itself has slowed down in comparison to the 80% Y/Y growth rate that was achieved in the same quarter last year. Although the company’s Instinct MI300 accelerators continue to gain traction, the management acknowledged that supply constraints and competitive pricing from Nvidia (NVDA) and Intel (INTC) are likely to limit near-term growth. Also, the cloud vendors are showing signs of more selective spending, with some prioritizing AI-enabled GPUs over general-purpose processors. That shift is putting pressure on AMD’s EPYC server processors, which are facing a more competitive environment.

In addition to that, AMD remains exposed to geopolitical risks, particularly in China, which is a key market for both its data center and client segments. The American export restrictions on advanced AI chips have already impacted AMD’s ability to sell its high-performance accelerators to Chinese customers. Although the management has said that it is working on compatible versions of the MI300 chips, the history shows that restricted SKUs often have significantly lower margins and limited demand. Also, the U.S. Department of Commerce is now tightening controls on semiconductor exports to several countries, creating uncertainty for future revenue streams across the globe. While AMD’s diversification into other, more reliable regions is helping the company offset some of the downside from restrictions, replacing high-value sales from China will likely be challenging and could weigh on profitability over the next few quarters.

Finally, AMD’s aggressive push into AI and specialty silicon is driving higher capital spending. In Q2, the spending on research and development reached $2 billion, or 21 percent of revenue, which reflects significant investments in new architectures and technology nodes. While R&D spending is important to remain competitive, it also has a negative impact on cash flow generation in the near term. In Q2, AMD generated only $1.1 billion in free cash flow compared to $1.5 billion last year, despite higher overall revenue. Also, the upcoming releases of Zen 5 processors and MI400 GPUs will require additional marketing and manufacturing commitments, further constraining cash flow.

With gross margin growth not expected to be significant in the near term, AMD may face difficulty balancing shareholder returns with the need to fund innovation at current levels. Because of that, we believe that the market has become overly excited about AMD’s latest growth and is ignoring the major risks that the company now faces.

Risk To Our Bearish Thesis

Several factors could undermine our bearish views. Faster-than-expected growth in MI300 shipments, combined with strong adoption of Zen 5-based EPYC processors, could accelerate growth in the data center market in the upcoming quarters. The gaming sector could also rebound if console refresh cycles accelerate, and consumer demand recovers in key markets.

In addition, easing export restrictions on more advanced chips or favorable geopolitical events could restore access to the high margin Chinese market. However, even under these optimistic scenarios, AMD will still face strong competitive pressure from Nvidia in AI accelerators and Intel in server processors, making it difficult to continue to expand margins.

Another factor that could challenge our pessimistic outlook is AMD’s diversified product portfolio. The company operates in the desktop, mobile, and could markets, which protects it from macroeconomic volatility. This broad range of activities could allow AMD to offset weakness in one segment with strength in another, leading to better-than-expected results in the following quarters.

The Real Value of AMD

Our valuation model, which is available below, was built when AMD’s stock was trading at $176.14 per share. In the model, we applied a 3 percent perpetual growth rate, consistent with the assumptions we typically use for comparable semiconductor peers. The forecast spans the next five years and incorporates inputs such as cash and debt, which have been taken from the most recent earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 10.82%. The discount rate was determined by combining AMD’s cost of equity and cost of debt. Debt costs were based on TTM results, while equity costs were calculated using the CAPM method, where the risk-free rate was 4.32%, a beta of 1.92, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

In our forecast, sales and EBIT estimates are broadly aligned with market consensus over the next five years. The model assumes a 15 percent tax rate, consistent with the level used in our previous valuation models for other semiconductor companies. The changes in net working capital in our model are expected to be 1% of sales, which is the rate that we also used when creating a valuation model for other semiconductor businesses. All other inputs are kept consistent with AMD’s historical performance.

Based on these assumptions, our model values AMD’s enterprise value at $101.5 billion. After adding cash and cash equivalents and subtracting total debt, we arrived at an equity value of $102 billion. On a per-share basis, this implies an intrinsic value of $62.91 per share, which represents a discount of about 64.3% to the current market price.

Final Thoughts

AMD remains one of the leading companies in high-performance computing, but its growth profile is slowing, and significant challenges lie ahead. Data center momentum is slowing, regulatory risks are rising, and capital expenditures are weighing on free cash flow. The current valuation leaves little room for execution error. For these reasons, we believe AMD’s risk-to-reward profile is unfavorable at current levels, and that is why the company remains a Sell for us.

data.title: How To Make Small-Cap Investing Less Risky
data.description:
It’s common knowledge that small-cap stocks are generally more volatile than their large-cap counterparts.

Some reasons proposed for this include lower liquidity, reduced analyst coverage, and more concentrated business models that lack diversification. Small companies are also more sensitive to economic cycles and interest rate changes, which can amplify market swings.

Seasoned small-cap investors may embrace this volatility, viewing it as an opportunity to deploy strategies like buy-write (covered call) approaches to harvest income from premium. But for long-term, buy-and-hold investors, the heightened day-to-day swings may be a deterrent.

Below, we explore two practical strategies, one technical and one fundamental, that could help reduce the risk of investing in small caps, without giving up too much upside.

Use Technical Indicators

Market timing gets criticized when it’s based on emotion or hunches. But using a systematic signal, like a moving average crossover, is a different approach entirely, one based on observable price trends rather than guesswork.

The chart below illustrates how an investor could use a simple rule based on the Russell 2000’s 50-day and 200-day simple moving averages (SMAs).

When the 50-day SMA crosses above the 200-day SMA, the investor would enter a long position in small caps. When the 50-day drops below the 200-day, they’d exit to cash.

This kind of rotation strategy can help limit drawdowns during extended downtrends. For example, an investor consistently executing trades based on this signal would have avoided a large portion of the early 2025 drawdown.

On the other hand, it’s important to recognize the trade-offs: moving average crossovers can lead to false signals, known as “whipsaws,” especially in sideways markets. This can increase turnover, transaction costs, and, if used in a taxable account, unwanted realized gains.

Still, for long-term investors looking to reduce small-cap volatility while maintaining upside participation during stronger periods, a rules-based technical filter like this could offer a viable framework.

Include Fundamental Screens

Another way to temper small-cap volatility is to focus on quality. In an investing context, quality refers to companies with strong fundamentals: consistent earnings, manageable debt, and efficient operations.

This becomes even more important in the small-cap space, where it’s easy for low-quality “junk” companies with negative earnings and weak balance sheets to find their way into passive indices.

A good benchmark to highlight the effect of a quality screen is the S&P SmallCap 600 Index. Unlike the Russell 2000, which only applies size and liquidity screens, the S&P 600 requires companies to have posted positive earnings in the most recent quarter and over the past four consecutive quarters.

As the following chart shows, this simple screen has historically translated into significantly stronger long-term returns than the Russell 2000.

You don’t need to rely on index rules to benefit from this approach. Active managers can apply more tailored and rigorous filters, such as positive free cash flow, high return on equity, dividend consistency, and net share buybacks, on top of the basic earnings checks.

These additional quality screens can help ensure your small-cap exposure tilts toward companies better equipped to handle market turbulence.

Putting It Together

The beauty of these technical and fundamental-based strategies for small caps is that they don’t need to be used in isolation. Investors can combine them.

For example, an investor might start by screening for a quality basket of small caps using fundamental checks like positive earnings, growing free cash flow, or shareholder-friendly actions like buybacks.

Then, instead of holding those stocks continuously, they could use the Russell 2000’s 50- and 200-day moving average crossover as a risk-on or risk-off signal to guide entry and exit timing.

Many active small-cap strategies already incorporate some variation of the above into how they build and manage portfolios. These funds can offer a seamless, all-in-one way to access the small-cap space with smarter risk controls built in.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. We have not sought, nor have we received, permission from any third-party to include their information in this article. Certain information contained in this document constitutes “forward-looking statements,” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “intend,” “continue” or “believe” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or other comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements. The information contained herein represents our subjective belief and opinions and should not be construed as investment, tax, legal, or financial advice.

Original Source: Infrastructure Capital Advisors

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HQH: Income Approach To Play Healthcare Recovery
data.description:
Introduction

As a blue-chip company in the healthcare sector, UnitedHealth Group's (UNH) stock went up a huge 12% on August 15. There was a big announcement that Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway purchased 5 million shares of UNH. The healthcare sector has been on my watch list for a while. The Berkshire's purchase appears to be a strong signal of a potential bottom for the struggling sector. It is also a good time for me to provide an update on my coverage of abrdn Healthcare Investors (HQH). HQH is an income CEF in the HC (healthcare) sector. It is an interesting approach to getting healthcare exposure. The fund currently pays a distribution of 12.40% and features a NAV discount of -9.36%. I had a BUY rating on HQH about a year ago, in anticipation of an HC sector recovery, which did not materialize. I will keep the buy rating this time and believe the worst might be seen in the healthcare sector, and a recovery in the next 12 months is possible based on the sector's cheap valuation and resilient fundamentals, as discussed below.

HQH Fund Overview

HQH is a closed-end fund managed by abrdn. It is considered a sector equity fund in the CEF universe, focusing on the healthcare industries, including biotech and healthcare services. The following are the top ten holdings of the fund:

Most of the top 10 are in the biotechnology industry, including the top two, Gilead and Amgen. Notice that UnitedHealth is one of the top holdings, and UNH and Abbott Laboratories are in the healthcare category.

The fund is not 100% in the equity holdings. There is a money market fund (the 8th on the list) of 2.45%. There is also about 6.5% allocated to the preferred stocks according to the fund information from its website. These are the dividend income choices.

The leverage is very small at 1.1% because of the equity fund nature. There is a 1.12% expense ratio, which is about normal for a CEF that is actively managed. What is more interesting is its income component. There is a dividend with a 12.4% yield issued every quarter. Many of the constituents offer good dividends around 3%, including the top two, Gilead and Amgen. 12.4% yield at the fund level will certainly attract more investors.

Given the rather negative market sentiment toward the HC sector, it is not surprising to see a -9.47% discount at the current trading price compared to its NAV, as seen below.

From the chart, one can also see the rather substantial NAV discount for the last three years or so.

HQH has been in the market for quite some time, as the fund was started in 1987. The current AUM is close to $1 billion ($981.85 million), making it a very solid equity CEF.

Key Recovery Signals Are Seen

Healthcare and biotech investing has been under very challenging conditions in 2025, on top of years of struggling, evidenced by the underperforming sector ETF, Health Care Select Sector SPDR (XLV). XLV's total return lagged behind the S&P 500 by 40% in the last two years or so.

On the other hand, the earnings seem to continue to grow as a whole. The following shows the earnings metrics for XLV, and used as a benchmark.

Notice that the growth metrics of revenue and income are all positive for 1-Y and 5-year averages. The -6.6% market cap 1-year reduction indicates the out-of-favor market sentiment for the sector.

From the recent Q2 report, many top holdings of HQH also reported satisfactory Q2 earnings in 2025. The following shows the leading examples:

  1. Amgen beat Q2 revenue and profit.
  2. Eli Lilly (LLY) Q2 was strong and lifted the guidance.
  3. Vertex reported a 12% increase in revenue for Q2, over Q2 2024

I think the overall revenue has been resilient for the last 5 years. The top names in the industry have shown some recovery in growing income again in 2025.

Berkshire's UNH purchase might be what is needed for market recovery

At the macro level, there are some significant headwinds and changes for the healthcare sector in 2025. The list includes the current vaccine skepticism, the US leadership changes in the FDA, the Make America Healthy movement, and the cost reduction of healthcare. These impacts could take some substantial time to digest and could serve as rather negative news for the market in the short run.

UnitedHealth has probably experienced the worst of all. At the company level, UNH had a CEO change, DoJ's Investigation, etc. As a result, the UNH stock price has been cut in half in 2025.

The healthcare sector has always been on my watch list because its overall demand has also been in a robust increase over time. As a result, it is an area where one can find decent long-term investment opportunities. I suspect 2025 to be the year we could experience the worst sentiment due to the macro challenges.

Berkshire's purchase of UnitedHealth Group shares could be the light at the end of the tunnel for interested investors. Buffett's value-centric and long-term investing approach is sending a strong signal that they see deep value in UNH shares. I believe it is also a strong signal for the potential good value of the sector. This could be what is needed to kick off a market recovery journey for it.

Sector Rotation May Get Started

I think one way to approach the HC recovery is the sector funds. HQH is a solid choice here. The deep NAV discount has shown a reasonable entry level for the price. The 12.4% yield can serve as a good income reward to compensate for the still highly volatile environment at the moment. To me, income is also a preferred approach for the long holdings in the portfolio.

Looking ahead, there is also a good possibility that a market rotation could happen because the broad market valuation is very high. The S&P 500 PE is very close to 30. Investors may find more attractive values in the underperforming sectors, such as the HC sector. XLV has a much lower P/E ratio at 24.5.

The following table shows the HQH top ownership distribution. Notice that there has been an increase in overall share ownership since May. I have also found it interesting that no negative changes are recorded in the top 20 list, and most (from FT/First Trust) have added shares in July. This could be a sign that money may start to flow into the healthcare space.

For long-term investors, the HQH portfolio should be a very solid foundation for a good total return. I have still been holding the HQH position opened a year ago and plan to add more shares in 2025 for both income and long-term return potential.

Risk Analysis

The market disconnect in the healthcare sector, as discussed earlier, may persist for a longer time. Some macro factors listed could see effects after years. There is no guarantee of the stock recovery; HQH may continue to suffer in a significant NAV discount and price loss, as shown for the past 3 years.

Conservative investors may want to be cautious and vigilant in the current healthcare environment, which is still very challenging. This is one of the reasons why I prefer the high-income approach for the time being. On the other hand, the dividend from some companies could be at risk if the profit margin of the sector is squeezed further. It could affect the dividend yield for HQH as well.

Closing Thoughts

Investing in the healthcare sector is a solid long-term approach, but it has produced poor results for investors in the last few years. 2025 could see some of the worst conditions due to macro headwinds. HQH CEF, with over 12% yield, offers an interesting alternative for investors who might expect the worst is behind the HC sector, signaled by the recent UNH share purchase from Berkshire.

data.title: Fed Rate Cuts Don't Mean Lower Bond Yields
data.description:
Whether Powell's Jackson Hole speech was dovish or hawkish misses the point. Ultimately, it doesn't matter: The bond market will have the final say, and the structure of the yield curve suggests it will come in the form of a bear steepener.

Softer Tone

Powell's speech offered very little that was new, except for taking it down a notch from the Fed minutes on Wednesday, by acknowledging that the BLS job data was not nearly as strong as previously believed. That led Powell to soften his stance on the health of the labor market and bring the inflation and labor debate into balance.

In the end, all he did was leave the door open for a rate cut in September, just in case the August job report turns out to be a disaster. However, Fed Fund futures were already pricing in all of this. September Fed Funds futures barely moved, slipping just one basis point to 4.24%, a move that was hardly noticeable.

One-year CPI inflation swaps didn't take to Powell's speech too kindly either, rising on the day to 3.35%. Keeping those inflation expectations well anchored at the upper end of the trading range from the past three years. A technical view of that chart still favors inflation expectations breaking out and moving higher.

The same is visible when examining CPI inflation swaps for five years, which are trading at the upper end of the trading range. Again, a technical view of the chart suggests that inflation expectations will continue to rise.

Rising inflation expectations will be a cue for bond yields to rise, as they have in the past. 10-year rates have traded right along with one-year and five-year CPI swaps for the past few years. One could even argue that the reason why 10-year and 30-year rates have been so contained and have not moved is because inflation expectations have been stuck in their own trading range.

The data continues to support the idea that inflation is unlikely to improve anytime soon. The Philadelphia Fed just reported its price paid index rose to its highest level since July 2022. If this data is reflected in the upcoming CPI or PPI reports, it's a good reason for inflation pricing to rise.

It Doesn't Matter

Ultimately, the Fed can adjust its stance on monetary policy and the direction of rates, and the market is free to do the same. Just because the Fed cuts rates at the front of the curve doesn't mean that the 10 and 30-year rates have to fall in kind. In fact, in the United Kingdom, Bank of England has been cutting rates regularly. However, the 30-year rate is at its highest point in decades, and from a technical standpoint, it appears that it too can go even higher.

Rate cuts from the ECB haven't stopped the Germany 10-year rate from rising, nor the spread with the Italian yields from contract. In fact, the spread between the Italian and German 10-year rates hasn't been this tight since before the 2008 financial crisis.

The bond markets may prove less forgiving than Powell and the Fed expect, especially given the risk of inflation being on the upside. Because if the market is OK with the current state of the job market or fears inflation more than the potential impacts of rising unemployment, the market will want to be compensated, and it will come in the form of rising rates at the back of the curve.

Outside of flat-out quantitative easing or yield curve control, there's not much that Powell or the Fed can do about that.

data.title: SFL Corporation: Weighing The Fallout Of A Dividend Cut And An Idle Hercules Rig
data.description:
Thesis

SFL Corp. (SFL) recently cut its quarterly dividend by 26% to $0.20/share. The company’s decision to cut the dividend was driven by the continued unemployment of the Hercules vessel and reduced revenues following the disposition of 21 container and dry bulk vessels.

Following the significant shakeup in the company’s fleet, SFL’s revenues have certainly gotten a makeover. However, despite the changes, the outlook for the idle Hercules rig continues to be the dominant factor for the company’s financials.

With this rig still unemployed but remaining in a hot-stacked condition, the company is approaching a neutral free cash flow position even after the reduced dividend. The harsh environment drilling market is expected to improve in 2026, but this market also faces competition from other service providers.

Due to the high dependence on the success or failure of the Hercules vessel, I am rating SFL as a HOLD.

A Busted Growth Thesis

When I last covered SFL, in the summer of 2024, a lot of things were going right for the company. The company had just secured robust long-term contracts on five tanker vessels, and the Hercules was beginning a very lucrative contract in Canda.

However, as the calendar turned to 2025, things started to head south. In fact, the stock has registered a total return of over -30% since I last discussed the company, making it one of my worst recommendations here on SA. Although this is highly disappointing, the only thing to do at this point is pick up the pieces and try to take an objective view of where to go from here.

Hercules Remains The Biggest Story, But The Story Had A Plot Twist

By now it should be highly evident that the financial success of SFL rests with the Hercules rig. This vessel can command a significant contract rate in the range of $300,000-$500,000/d. As recently as Q4 2024, this vessel was contracted at the high end of this range, accounting for nearly 20% of the company’s revenue.

Fast forward to today, and this vessel has been idle for the vast majority of the year while it sits in a hot-stacked condition in search of its next contract. This situation keeps the vessel’s operating costs elevated while revenue generation has fallen to zero.

The company’s management is still in search of new work for this rig but holds out hope for promising activity levels developing for harsh environment semi-submersibles in 2026. However, the Hercules will be pitted against competing rigs from key competitors such as Transocean (RIG), Noble (NE), Seadrill (SDRL), and Valaris (VAL). These companies also have significant white space in their contract books for similar vessels, although overall competitiveness can vary greatly in this niche area of offshore drilling.

While in the hot stacked configuration, the company expects this vessel to log an operating cost of $80,000/d. The company has elected to absorb this additional cost as it sees this as the lesser of two evils compared to cold stacking the unit. SFL has also taken this downtime to further upgrade the rig and catch up on neglected maintenance that remains outstanding from the rig's unfortunate return from Seadrill.

Ideally, these efforts should ensure that the Hercules vessel is ready to go should the market call for its services. But that “should” remains the crux of the problem. 2025 has not been kind to oil prices and has caused many companies to pause and reassess their development plans.

The target market for the Hercules is in Norway, where the vessel is currently stacked. The current outlook has activity projected to pick up by mid-2026. However, it would not surprise me to see some of this activity get delayed now that OPEC+ has fully opened its doors by bringing 2.2 million barrels per day of projection into the market in just a few short months.

Despite the timing uncertainty, there will always be the notion of getting paid to wait until the Hercules rig picks up a contract. This would be playing the market for an eventual rebound, and that strategy has merit. On the other hand, I feel this strategy is probably not in line with the rest of SFL’s portfolio of steady long-term contracts. This may make this play unappealing to the yield-seeking investor.

Changes In Cash Flow After Trimming The Portfolio

Beyond the situation regarding the Hercules rig, SFL reported a significant alteration in its fleet composition in the second quarter earnings release. The company parted ways with 21 vessels, including 12 dry bulk carriers and 8 container ships. The majority of these vessels were late in life, allowing the company to recycle capital into renewing the fleet.

Disposing of these vessels clearly will impact revenues and operating expenses, so it’s worth taking a look at potential future cash flows. For a rough estimate, I have assumed that earnings for the dry bulk segment go to essentially zero now that the fleet has shrunk from 15 vessels to 3. I have also modeled in a 25% reduction in earnings from the container segment.

These estimates are rather crude but are at least reasonable and likely a bit conservative. In this way, I now expect the company to generate breakeven cash flows after paying both the dividend and capital investments into the fleet.

In the absence of additional revenue from the Hercules vessel, the company will need to find accretive deals to provide additional margin to positive free cash flow. At quarters end, the company had $156 million in cash on the balance sheet. In addition, the company received $150 million via the sale of 12 of the company’s dry bulk vessels. The combined liquidity should be substantive enough to broker a deal, but opportunities may be in short supply in the near term given the current volatility in the market.

Valuation

After accounting for the reduced revenue from the previously discussed vessel sales, I expect EBITDA to reduce to roughly $95 million moving forward. When annualized, this generates an EV:EBITDA value of roughly 9.5x. This is a significant step up from the trailing multiple of 7.6x.

Effectively, the company’s debt load dwarfs the market cap and cash balance. Because of this, a relatively small change in EBITDA has a notable change in the company’s valuation. In my view, SFL doesn’t warrant a premium multiple just because it has a lucrative yield. As noted in the free cash flow discussion, the dividend has minimal margin. This makes the investment somewhat at risk to future dividend cuts.

Risks

The direction of global oil prices will drive the overall timeline for the Hercules vessel. Based on OPEC+ production increases, global oil markets are in a state of surplus. Global demand growth will likely not catch up to these production increases until mid-to-late 2026.

Therefore, any economic distress or further downward pressure on oil prices could impact investment in new oil projects. The result for SFL could be longer idle times for this key asset. The longer this holds true, the longer the company sits in a somewhat precarious neutral free cash flow position.

Investor Key Takeaways

  1. The next window of opportunity for the Hercules rig is likely mid-2026, but this is subject to change based on the performance of global oil markets.
  2. The changes to the dividend payout and fleet structure put the company at a near-breakeven free cash flow profile.
  3. The resulting EBITDA reduction degrades the company’s valuation and is expensive for a company facing moderate operational challenges and a high debt load.
  4. The risk versus reward aspects of a position in SFL are oddly skewed between long-term contracts and the dependence on the Hercules vessel. Investors seeking stable income may be better suited to other securities.
  5. I rate SFL as a HOLD due to an expensive valuation and moving target on recontacting of the Hercules rig.

data.title: ACP: 15%+ Yield, Discount, But Long-Term Underperformance
data.description:
Will the Fed cut rates in September? If it does, existing fixed-income vehicles should get a boost. Of course, the market has been waiting for quite some time for a rate cut, with the hope that the cut(s) will benefit several industries and sectors.

The Abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund (NYSE:ACP) yields over 15% and is trading at a discount to NAV. It's down slightly so far in 2025.

When we last covered ACP in January '25, we rated it a Sell due to reduced prospects for a rate cut in 2025. Even with increased potential for a September rate cut from the Fed, we maintain our Sell rating on ACP due to several reasons, which we'll discuss below.

Fund Profile

"ACP aims to seek a high level of current income with a secondary objective of capital appreciation. The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objectives by opportunistically investing primarily in debt and loan instruments of issues that operate in a variety of industries and geographic regions. The Fund may invest, without limitation, in credit obligations that are rated below investment grade.

The Fund's policy is to provide common shareholders with a stable monthly distribution out of current income, supplemented by realized capital gains and, to the extent necessary, paid-in capital, which is a non-taxable return of capital." (ACP site)

With $767M in Net Assets, ACP has 152 holdings and 525K in daily volume. Management uses leverage, 28% as of 6/30/25, which added 2.20% to its expense ratio of 4.44%. Its leverage is considerably higher than the 15% it had in early October '24. ACP began in January 2011.

Holdings

Consumer Discretionary continues to be ACP's highest allocation, followed by Materials, at 13.7%, which is up 110 basis points vs. 11/30/24. Financials have increased by 360 basis points, to 11.7%, while Consumer Staples have risen 260 basis points. Capital Goods have declined 170 basis points, and Energy has dropped 110 basis points since 11/30/24.

As of 6/30/25, 79% of ACP's portfolio was allocated to maturities of 0-5 years, down from 82.3% at 11/30/24, while 18% was in 5-10 year maturities, up from 14%.

Non-investment-grade securities dominate the portfolio, with BB-rated holdings at ~20%, B-rated at 60.7%, and CCC at 14.7%.

ACP's top 10 holdings are each just 2.3% or less of its total portfolio, with the top 10 accounting for just 19% of its holdings. There was quite a lot of turnover since 11/30/24, with 7 new positions in the top 10 holdings: Bracken Midco, 888 Acquisitions, Summer BC US Holdco, Staples, Sherwood Financing, Fiesta Purchaser, and Wolseley Group.

The US, at ~28%, and the UK, at ~26%, continue to be ACP's top 2 regional exposures, with the US down 350 basis points and the UK up 270 basis points since 11/30/24.

The US Dollar remains the top currency exposure, at 55.7%, followed by the EURO, at 29.6%, and the British Pound, at 14.7%.

Dividends

At $5.96, ACP yields 15/60%. It pays $0.0775 monthly and should go ex-dividend next on ~9/19/25, with a 9/30/25 pay date. It has paid $.0775 since December '24, when the monthly distribution was cut from $.10.

ACP has a 5-year dividend growth average of -3.84%, with cuts in 2020, 2021, and 2024.

The pace of NII picked up dramatically in the 6 months ending 4/30/25, hitting $32.56M—that's up ~62% vs. $39.65M in the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024 on an annualized basis. Realized losses improved ~23% vs. the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024, on an annualized basis. Unrealized losses, however, swung from a $58M gain in the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024 to -$36M in the 6 months ended 4/30/25.

Distributions for the 6 months ended 4/30/25 were $61M, with NII only covering ~53%, vs. ~48% in the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024. ACP's asset base shrank ~$74M in the 6 months ended 4/30/25, to $746M.

Taxes

Distributions for the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024 were characterized as ~63% Return of Capital (ROC). ROC offers a tax deferral advantage, but it does decrease your tax basis, which will impact your tax bite if you sell your shares.

Performance

As of 7/31/25, ACP has outperformed the Morningstar US CEF Senior Loan category in price and NAV over the past 3- and 6-month periods and 1-year period. It also outperformed it on an NAV basis over the past 3-year period, but lagged on a price basis due to its decline in 2022.

Longer term, ACP has lagged the category over the past 5- and 10-year periods.

ACP has outperformed its category so far in 2025 on a price and NAV basis and also did so in 2023, 2020, 2019, and 2017.

Risks

Currency Risk: As ACP holds ~45% of its portfolio in foreign currencies, adverse changes in currency valuations could pressure its value. In 2025, the US dollar has fallen ~12% vs. the Euro and ~7% vs. the British Pound.

With the dollar being ACP's highest exposure, at ~56%, the Euro at 29.6%, and the British Pound at 14.7%, ACP is getting some hedging benefit vs. the dollar's decline.

Interest Rate Risk: Rising interest rates will pressure fixed income vehicles, as evidenced in ACP's 22.5% loss in 2022, a year of rising rates.

Valuations

Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day.

As of the 8/20/25 close, ACP was trading at a 2.93% discount to its NAV/share. While that's not as deep as its 1-year average discount of 3.75%, it's deeper than its 3-year 2.05% discount and its 5-year 0.64% discount. Note that ACP's NAV/share was $23.83 at its inception in 2011.

Parting Thoughts

While ACP's 2.9% discount is deeper than its 3- and 5-year average pricing, the decline in its NAV/share, its underperformance over the past 5- and 10-year periods, and its history of dividend cuts cause us to rate it a Sell.

If you're interested in other high-yield vehicles, we cover them every Friday and Sunday in our articles.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: PayPal: Don't Let The Steep Selloff Go To Waste
data.description:
PayPal: Fed Chair Powell Signals A September Rate Cut

A much-needed (relief) rally for investors of PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL), kudos to the dovish posture undertaken by Fed Chair Jerome Powell in the clearest signal yet of a September interest rate cut. I think it's still too early for us to ascertain whether it could be just another relief rally before the momentum fizzle out, or a harbinger to a more decisive breakout to a new high. Why?

Well, it's not as if the market hasn't been anticipating a rate cut starting in September. As a matter of fact, investors had baked slightly below four cuts over the next 12 months, aligning with the bullish optimism that drove the market to a new recent peak.

Nevertheless, the same cannot really be said of PYPL investors, as the stock of the fintech leader continues to struggle for traction. While it has also received a welcomed bullish jolt as I pen this article, it remains far below its January 2025 highs, demonstrating the substantive lack of conviction from the market about its recovery prospects. Clearly, as the S&P 500 (SPX, SPY) and its financial sector peers (XLF) notched new recent heights, PayPal investors must be lamenting about just what it would take for the market to regain confidence in the fintech leader once again.

PayPal: Near-Term Uncertainties Vs. Long-Term Growth Optionalities

In my last PayPal update, I reminded investors that the company boasts a two-sided merchant and consumer platform. While the valuation de-rating of its growth premium was justified, the worst of its decline appears to be over, behooving me to take a more positive stance amidst its challenges.

Facing the twin threats of intense competition and a potentially more pervasive economic slowdown, PayPal arguably has its work cut out, as it navigates transformative changes in the fintech and digital payments marketplace.

Notably, I believe astute and observant investors have likely scrutinized the incursions into PayPal's higher-margin Branded segment. Its previous venture into the less profitable (but higher growth potential) Unbranded opportunities was designed as the primary response to rescue its growth premium. However, as PayPal's Q2 earnings release has unveiled, it has decided to reset the balance between volume and margins. In that aspect, management has made it clear that it will continue to shift away from lower-margin Unbranded volume growth opportunities to better secure its transaction margins. However, that has also affected its Unbranded volume growth, which delivered flat YoY growth in Q2, highlighting the immense challenges in that growth/volume reset.

With that in mind, I wouldn't fault the market for taking the safer route, as it contemplated management's caution on consumer spending, while also taking into account the ambiguities engendered by tariff-related impacts. Hence, I thought Powell's speech at the Jackson Hole symposium lent substantially more clarity into the policy inclinations based on their current data points. And, I believe it's increasingly clear that the balance of employment and inflation risks have skewed further to the downside, threatening the jobs outlook. In addition, Powell's address implies that it's not immediately clear that the tariffs could lead to an inflationary spiral, threatening the economic outlook beyond 2026. Hence, it does seem like we should see a more sustained bottom for PYPL stock even as it treads water toward the rest of 2025.

However, while economic risks threatening PayPal's core e-commerce and omnichannel growth vectors should be mitigated, the emerging threats from stablecoins should persist. Thanks to the clarity afforded by the passing of the GENIUS Act, PayPal has also been moving rapidly to participate in the stablecoin ecosystem, but it's still too nascent for us to assess whether it's enough to carve out a long-term dominance against well-established peers.

Legacy payments players such as Visa (V) are also priming to partake in what could be transformative changes in the digital payments ecosystem, which could also upend their business models. Hence, the long-term outlook for PayPal could still be murky, as it wrangles with the ambivalence obscuring more favorable forecasts over the next five to ten years, which could jeopardize its valuation upgrade/re-rating potential moving ahead.

PayPal: Margins Still Expected To Stay Solid

Nevertheless, while I don't have a firm grasp on whether PayPal could circumvent these intense headwinds successfully, I also think it's crucial for us to give credence to its fundamentally strong platform.

Note that while the Unbranded payments have been somewhat deprioritized in favor of margin accretion, it should help the fintech company pave the way for a medium-term margin recovery.

And I believe it's noteworthy that, in spite of the current challenges afflicting its revenue growth and transaction margins trajectory, it's still expected to be substantially free cash flow profitable.

Consequently, it has afforded PYPL to continue supporting aggressive share repurchases (current annualized run rate: $6B). I acknowledge that the growth optionalities in stablecoin, crypto, Agentic Commerce, and the newly launched PayPal World integration are considered nascent and might not materially move the needle in the short term. Yet, I believe we need to give management more time to overcome the near-term hurdles of resetting its volume growth strategy, while executing the go-to-market in these new vectors, and also fending off threats from opportunistic big tech and platform companies keen on encroaching further into PYPL's turf.

Buy Or Sell PYPL Stock?

And also, I can truly comprehend the disappointment experienced by PYPL investors, as the stock's underperformance against XLF since the start of 2025 doesn't seem to augur well. PayPal is, without a doubt, facing monumental challenges aiming to upend its platform moat, as volume growth stalled.

Hence, with the introduction of more well-endowed stablecoin competitors seeking to overturn the advantages enjoyed by the legacy players (and that includes PYPL), it has arguably compounded the pre-existing headwinds that have afflicted its growth prospects.

Yet, I have also observed that PYPL/XLF has still managed to hold the current defense line, suggesting all isn't lost yet. Interestingly, PYPL's underperformance against XLF hasn't collapsed to a new nadir, suggesting that accumulation at the current levels seems apt.

And, with the stock printing a forward PEG ratio of 1.32 (aligned closely with the financial median of 1.26), the premium that was previously accorded to the stock is arguably long gone. Hence, I assess that patient investors with a longer-term horizon to wait out the current consolidation might find the current setup appealing to buy more shares.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: NIO Stock Jumps: New Model Changes Everything
data.description:
My Thesis Update

It's been a while since I covered NIO Inc. (NIO) stock last time, but it looks like my upgrade in April of this year has been playing out quite well for the past few weeks:

What's been driving the stock price to the upside was NIO's continued operational shift that I was pointing to last time, and as a key part of that shift, we saw the news that the firm unveiled its new model [ES8], which is cheaper versus their previous models, and it's considered to compete with others in the Chinese car market. I think this is exactly what we can call a game-changer for NIO's prospective operational growth because the high price marks of its past models were the main explanation for the business expansion in the past. Now it's likely to change, and NIO seems to have the needed resources to make it happen. I'm reiterating my "Buy," waiting for new highs ahead.

My Updated Reasoning

For Q1 2025, NIO delivered 42,094 vehicles, which was a 40.1% YoY increase, leading to sales of ~$1.67 billion (+21.5% YoY). The market was expecting more, and the same thing is true regarding NIO's bottom-line improvements (the firm missed the adjusted EPS estimate by ~5 cents):

It's hard to say that analysts and general market participants were expecting a strong beat from NIO because, statistically speaking, it would have been a clear outlier to what everyone's already got used to.

Although the consensus systematically fails to assign close-to-reality expectations, I think that NIO did deliver some good news in its Q1 release. For example, I liked the fact that their vehicle margin improved to 10.2% from 9.2% in Q1 2024, and it made the gross profit margin soar up to 7.6% (from 4.9% last year), so the key margin almost doubled on a YoY basis, which was great to see. Having excluded Lucid Auto (LCID) from the peer group because its COGS exceeds revenues, I see that NIO's GP trend is generally in sync with what other Chinese automakers show.

As you can see, the lag with the peers is still quite high, and NIO's GP margin should at least triple to be comparable to those of BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF)(OTCPK:BYDDY) or Li Auto (LI). It's not an easy thing to do, and it explains the persistent underperformance of NIO's stock versus BYD and others.

But things started to change recently, with NIO coming out as a strong leader even despite the massive misses on the consensus estimates in recent quarters.

Why so?

I think the firm's strategic re-focus has started to work out better than many skeptics had been expecting before. First, the GP margin is already on the rise, and the strength of the rise looks promising in itself. Second, the company recently rolled out a new ES8 model, whose price is set "lower than the starting price for the Tesla (TSLA) Model Y L six-seat SUV" (at ~$58K, which is going to be a 25% reduction from the previous generation).

The redesigned ES8 expands from a mid-large to a full-size SUV to become the largest battery-electric SUV in China. Built on NIO's 12 full-stack technological capabilities, it was highlighted by the company as combining an expressive third-generation "Design for Success" philosophy with premium materials, bespoke interiors, and airplane-style zero-gravity seating configurations.

Source: Seeking Alpha News

That was a very important game-changer because a lower-priced full-size SUV is exactly what NIO needed to broaden its product line and re-accelerate its delivery growth in the next few years. Luxury SUVs and SUVs in general are one of the highest-growth auto markets that gain market share within the auto mix constantly.

It's great for NIO to be there and offer an optimally priced vehicle to its customers. It's adding more chances for NIO to get a much higher gross profit margin as they keep scaling on this new front, especially taking into account that over the past few years, the firm gained enough technical expertise to develop some of the needed chips in-house (that's a major cost savings versus Nvidia Orin X). And they can also save up on R&Ds thanks to R&D cost sharing across multiple models with their 900V platform.

William Li explained how the cost was saved to achieve such a cost reduction, mentioning a few main aspects. Nio renegotiated the cost with suppliers, lowering the procurement cost. R&D cost was spread across multiple models, sharing the core technologies such as the 900 V charging platform, rear motor, and in-house developed chip. The ES8 also underwent some material adjustments with a reduction of aluminium content.

Source: From CarNewsChina [August 22, 2025]

I'd expect something close to at least 15-17% cost savings for the upcoming Q2 (a bit higher at the midpoint vs. the management's guidance), and it's going to help NIO return to double-digit gross profit margin in Q2 (and hopefully beyond that). NIO can break even on EBITDA, assuming a) monthly deliveries of over 50,000 units, b) vehicle gross margins of 17-18%, and c) well-thought-out OPEX/CAPEX management.

And going into the fourth quarter, we are going to introduce our all new ES8, the next generation ES8, which is a highly competitive product. With that, we believe that in Q4, for the NIO brand, the monthly delivery will be around 25,000 units. That's around 20% year-over-year growth from last year's Q4, which was around 20,000 units a month. In that case, while improving the sales volume, we are also going to improve our vehicle gross margin significantly for the NIO brand to be above 20% in Q4.

Source: From NIO's Q1 FY2025 earnings call

It's too early to tell about the EPS (even on an adjusted basis), but with the recent events in mind, I'm not so sure that the current EPS breakeven on FY2028 is correctly reflecting the real odds. I think NIO can now make it a bit faster.

At 1-1.2x of forward sales, NIO's market cap should be $16.2-19.44 billion for me to call it fairly valued (again, with the core assumption that NIO's profitability prospects are now closer to reality than ever before).

This gives us an upside of 13.7-36.5% to the current stock price, even after Friday's surge of over 14%.

Risks And Your Takeaway

I admit some fragility to my bullish thesis on NIO because if its new ES8 model doesn't gain as much popularity as, for example, Li Auto's Lixiang model range did in the past few years, the firm's operational struggles are likely to come back to their past questionable conditions, not to mention the market narrative around the stock. All investors should keep in mind that NIO's plans to break even can be shifted again and again, and the firm will likely need a few rounds of external capital injections (hence dilutions) to finance its strategic plans. Yes, they hold over $2.7 billion in cash on the balance sheet, but their TTM-based EBIT is negative ~$3.15 billion, so another share sale is a question of "when", not "if."

Anyway, NIO is still a high-growth stock, and it has just confirmed that with both the ES8 roll-out (high growth in future sales/earnings) and a massive bullish reaction in terms of the stock price action. I think that reaction is there to stay with us for some time: Assuming the stock can reprice to reasonable levels on the P/S side, the stock price can gain another 14-37% in a matter of weeks, if not days. And if the company starts to show higher GP margins, as I expect, the stock price upside will be much higher than that.

I decided to keep my "Buy" on NIO in active mode for now.

Good luck with your investments!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Whirlpool: We Maintain Our Buy Rating Despite The Macroeconomic Headwinds
data.description:
Whirlpool (NYSE:WHR) is one of the most well-known household brands in the world. They are famous for their home appliances and related products, which they manufacture and sell internationally.

We started covering the company back in 2022 and have published 5 articles since then. Initially we had a neutral view on the firm, but later we came more bullish due to the attractive valuation based on dividend discount models and improving fundamentals.

Today our goal is to give an updated view on WHR's business, taking into account the changes in the macroeconomic environment, the firm's valuation, as well as the latest quarterly results, published late July.

Macroeconomic environment and quarterly results

The current macroeconomic environment and its direction do not appear to be positive for WHR's business.

Consumer confidence

First, let us take a look at consumer confidence in the United States. The following chart depicts how consumer sentiment has changed over the past decade.

Consumer confidence is a leading economic indicator, which is often used to gauge how consumer spending is likely to change in the near future. Poor sentiment often means that people are less certain about their financial situation and less likely to spend on durable discretionary goods, like the ones WHR is selling.

The low consumer confidence is already visible in WHR's latest earnings. Due to the low demand globally, net sales decreased by 5.4% year-over-year.

These headwinds are expected to last for the rest of 2025 at least. And they are clearly reflected in both the management's guidance and in analyst estimates.

Guidance figures were substantially revised to the lower side in the previous quarter for each segment:

The cash flow guidance was also revised lower, although we are still expecting minimal growth compared to the prior year.

Analysts also see WHR's sales falling for the coming years, along with their earnings.

Last, but not least, we would also like to point out that the low demand, combined with increasing inventory, leads to a decreasing inventory turnover, which may potentially lead to obsolete inventory and eventually to lower profitability in the future.

Tariffs

Tariffs were in the spotlight for most of the year and investors were having difficulties gauging how the constantly changing policies might influence the players in the industry and their financial performance.

WHR seems to be well-positioned to withstand the negative impacts of the tariffs, as 80% of their production is handled by domestic U.S. players. In contrast, WHR's competitors are largely importers, importing mainly from China and Mexico.

Home sales

In the firm's latest quarterly report, we can also read about the correlation between the demand for WHR's products and existing home sales. The following slide shows that existing home sales are below historical averages, driven primarily by the rapid interest rate increases. The company does not expect the demand for existing homes to pick up significantly in the near term; therefore, they also expect lower demand for their products in the coming months and quarters.

Interest rate

Interest rate is a significant driver of both consumer confidence and the demand for homes and discretionary products. The Fed increased interest rates rapidly in 2022 as a response to growing inflation.

Although the peak is likely behind us, the Fed is not likely to cut rates aggressively. Inflation in the recent months increased again, which is likely leading to an even lower likelihood of a near term rate cut.

All in all, we believe that the current macroeconomic environment is creating significant headwinds for not only WHR's business, but for the whole industry. These headwinds are not likely to ease in the near term.

Valuation

To assess WHR's fair value, we are going to look at a set of traditional price multiples today. The following summarizes these multiples:

Based on these metrics, we can see that WHR's stock appears to be trading at a significant discount compared to both the consumer discretionary sector median, and its own historic valuation metrics.

On one hand, we understand the reason for the discount, as highlighted above, poor consumer sentiment, a struggling housing market, the uncertainty around upcoming interest rate cuts are all creating headwinds for WHR in the near term. On the other hand, the current valuation is difficult to ignore for long-term investors. If someone is ready to stick with WHR in the long run, we believe that eventually decreasing interest rates are likely to boost both consumer confidence and the housing market, leading to higher demand for WHR's products. As it seems now the firm is also likely to benefit from the U.S. tariff policies. Last, but not least we need to mention that WHR is also a responsible dividend payer, paying $0.9 quarterly, which corresponds to an annual yield of roughly 4.1% currently. Although it is a significant step back from the amount paid in the previous years - $1.75 quarterly, we understand the rationale behind the dividend cut, and agree with this capital allocation strategy.

All in all, we believe that WHR is attractive from a valuation point of view at the current price levels.

Conclusions

The macroeconomic environment is currently causing significant headwinds to WHR's business. Poor consumer confidence, low existing home sales and high interest rates are all negatively impacting the demand for WHR's products and therefore the firm's financial performance.

The last quarterly results also show sales decline, and the 2025 guidance was also revised lower across all metrics.

On the other hand, WHR may benefit from the tariff policies as roughly 80% of their production is done domestically.

From a valuation point of view, WHR's stock appears appealing. The stock is selling at a discount compared to both the sector median and the firm's own historic metrics. We believe that the macroeconomic factors are already priced in.

For these reasons, we believe that WHR could be an attractive stock to buy at the current valuation for investors, who have a longer-term focus.
